reasoning_summary: Run 20260421_185710_bf16_gemm_v1_823cbff4 at 24.697857 ms is the best result on the current frontier branch so far and validates the grouped_rows restore. Registers stayed flat at 202 and runtime dropped by another 0.76 ms, so the launch-order recovery was worth keeping. The remaining picture is now a compact 22,016 B PTX hot-band surface with barrier at 6.42% and long_scoreboard at 5.52%, still only 16.58% active warps and about 0.53 ms behind the best known run on this repo. That makes a narrow wait/sync cadence trim the best next move, because it targets the leading local stall without reopening stage-count risk. I rank shared-offset hoisting second as a low-risk hot-loop control cleanup that can shave warp-local address work on the same active 128x128 surface. I keep the writer-safe 256x128 / 64x64 reopen third as the higher-ceiling structural branch once the local compact PTX loop has been exploited.
family_audit: accept: the compact two-stage PTX surface with grouped_rows=4 is now the active accepted base at 24.697857 ms and should not be rolled back.
accept: prioritize narrow hot-loop exploitation on the compact PTX path before reopening stage depth or replaying stale restore candidates.
defer_but_keep_open: keep the writer-safe 256x128 / 64x64 structural branch queued after the local compact PTX loop is squeezed further.

directions.name: Trim The Compact Two-Stage PTX Wait-Sync Cadence On The 22016B Shared Surface
directions.hypothesis: Now that grouped_rows and register pressure are back on the compact winning surface, the most actionable remaining tax is CTA handoff cadence itself. A narrow retime of wait_group and __syncthreads placement on the 22,016 B PTX loop should reduce barrier without reopening the stage-count or shared-memory regressions from the failed round-6 branch.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Barrier and CTA handoff overhead are the clearest remaining local bottlenecks on the accepted compact PTX base.
directions.risk: Moderate. This stays entirely on the compact two-stage PTX loop, but it still touches fragile synchronization semantics.
ranking_notes: This is the strongest next move because barrier is now the largest explicit local stall on the accepted compact PTX surface.
It preserves the winning 202-register and 22,016 B shared-memory footprint instead of reopening 3-stage family risk.
If it cannot move the 24.70 ms base, the local compact PTX surface is likely close to exhausted.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the trim requires increasing shared memory above 22,016 B or materially increasing registers above the current 202-register budget.
expected_local_changes: Retune wait_group and __syncthreads placement inside the compact two-stage PTX hot-band loop.
Keep stage depth at 2 and shared memory at 22,016 B.
Do not change grouped_rows or launch geometry in the first pass.

directions.name: Hoist 128x128 Hot-Band Shared Offsets Out Of The Steady-State Loop
directions.hypothesis: Both active 128x128 hot-band kernels still carry invariant warp-local shared offset arithmetic through the K loop even though row and column warp positions are fixed for the CTA lifetime. Hoisting those offsets once per warp should shave integer address work and reduce control dilution on a surface that is already close to the branch best.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Warp-local shared-pointer arithmetic and hot-loop control overhead are small but still relevant on the accepted compact PTX base.
directions.risk: Low. This is a bounded control-overhead cleanup and should not change tile geometry or shared-memory budgeting.
ranking_notes: This is the cleanest low-risk exploit path once the compact PTX base is already close to the best branch result.
It attacks loop-carried overhead without touching synchronization semantics or tiling.
It ranks below dir_01 because barrier is still a larger measured tax than pointer arithmetic.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the hoist materially grows registers or requires per-iteration predicates to be replaced with larger control scaffolding.
expected_local_changes: Hoist warp-local A and B shared offsets once per warp before the K loop in the active 128x128 hot-band kernels.
Leave synchronization, grouped_rows, and tiling unchanged.
Keep the change limited to loop-invariant address setup.

directions.name: Reopen The Writer-Safe 256x128 64x64-Warp Hot-Band Branch From The Current PTX Base
directions.hypothesis: If the compact PTX branch remains stuck near 24.7 ms after one more local exploit pass, the remaining gap is probably geometric. The source already contains a writer-safe 256x128 / 64x64 path aligned with the persistent human guidance, so that branch should stay queued as the higher-ceiling alternative.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The 128x128 PTX surface may still be capped by occupancy and warp-reuse geometry even after local loop cleanup.
directions.risk: High. This is a broader structural reopen that touches geometry, ownership, and export path together.
ranking_notes: This remains the highest-ceiling structural branch once the compact PTX surface is locally exhausted.
The writer-safe 256x128 path is already in the tree, so the reopen is bounded even though it is still risky.
It stays rank 3 because the current base is close enough to the branch best that another local exploit pass is still justified.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the reopen requires reviving a previously broken ownership or export path instead of the writer-safe 256x128 surface already present in the source.
expected_local_changes: Re-enable the writer-safe 256x128 / 64x64 PTX hot-band branch.
Keep async-copy staging and shared-memory reuse intact on the larger CTA geometry.
Leave non-hot-band routing unchanged in the first pass.

notes: Round 9 diagnosis emitted from the new compact PTX accepted base after grouped_rows restoration.
